metadata.career_relevance: Razorpay processes 500M+ payment requests per month. Without rate limiting: one compromised merchant key floods the system, other merchants suffer. Bucket4j implements token bucket algorithm: each merchant gets 100 req/min; overage → 429 Too Many Requests with Retry-After header. After this topic, you can protect any Spring Boot API from abuse, brute force, and DDoS.

hook.real_world_problem:
Razorpay payment API without rate limiting:
```
9:00am: Attacker scripts 10,000 payment attempts/min with stolen card numbers
→ Payment service CPU: 100%
→ Real merchant payments: timeout
→ Flipkart Diwali sale: 502 Bad Gateway for 20 minutes
→ Revenue loss: ₹50 lakh/minute

With Bucket4j rate limiting:
→ Each API key: 1000 req/min (token bucket)
→ Attacker's key: throttled to 1000/min (harmless)
→ Real merchants: unaffected (each has their own bucket)
→ Service healthy under attack
```
hook.what_you_will_build: Bucket4j rate limiter per API key/merchant, with Redis for distributed rate limiting across multiple service instances.

visual_aid.description: Token bucket diagram: bucket fills at 100 tokens/min. Each request consumes 1 token. Normal traffic: bucket stays full. Attack: bucket empties → 429 returned. Distributed: Redis bucket shared across 3 pods (each pod reads/writes same Redis key). Rate limit headers shown: X-RateLimit-Limit=100, X-RateLimit-Remaining=45, Retry-After=60.
visual_aid.alt_text: Token bucket rate limiting with Redis for distributed state across pods

worked_example.scenario: Rate limit Razorpay payment API: 100 req/min per merchant, 1000 req/min per IP for unauthenticated.
worked_example.problem_statement:
1. Bucket4j filter: per-merchant rate limiting
2. 429 response with Retry-After header
3. @SpringBootTest: 101st request returns 429

step_by_step_solution.details: Bucket starts with 100 tokens. Each request: consume 1 token. If bucket has tokens: request passes. If empty: 429. Bucket refills: 100 tokens per minute (greedy = spread evenly, or burst all at once). Merchant A's bucket is independent of Merchant B's bucket.

things_to_notice: Per-merchant bucket: each merchant has their own token bucket. One merchant's DDoS doesn't affect other merchants. Compare with global rate limiting: one attacker kills service for all.
Retry-After header: tells clients when to retry. RFC 7231 standard. Well-behaved clients respect this and back off. Include in 429 responses to prevent busy-waiting.

Greedy vs interval refill: Greedy = distributes tokens evenly over time (smooths traffic). Interval = refills all tokens at interval boundary (allows bursts). Greedy is usually better for payment APIs.

Idempotency keys: for payment APIs, clients should send X-Idempotency-Key with each request. If a 429 causes a retry, the idempotency key ensures the payment isn't created twice. Store idempotency keys in Redis with 24h TTL.

industry_examples.use_case: Per API key: 300 req/min (standard), 1000 req/min (enterprise). Exceeded → 429 with error.code='rate_limit_exceeded'. Client libraries: automatically retry with exponential backoff + Retry-After header. Alert: any merchant hitting 80% of their limit → proactive upgrade offer.

applications.interview_relevance: 'How do you implement rate limiting in Spring Boot?' → Bucket4j with token bucket algorithm. One Bucket per identifier (API key, IP, user). OncePerRequestFilter: tryConsume(1) → allow or 429. For distributed: Redis-backed Bucket4j with Redisson. Headers: X-RateLimit-Limit, X-RateLimit-Remaining, Retry-After.
applications.real_world_workflow: Rate limiting workflow: (1) identify limiting key (API key > user ID > IP), (2) configure token bucket (Bucket4j), (3) filter in filter chain (before business logic), (4) 429 with Retry-After on overflow, (5) Prometheus: rate(rate_limit_exceeded[5m]) > 0 → alert for abuse detection.

common_gaps.what_students_get_wrong: Using in-memory Caffeine bucket in a multi-pod deployment. Each pod allows N req/min, so total = N × pods.
common_gaps.remediation: For multi-pod: use Redis-backed Bucket4j (Bucket4jRedisson or bucket4j-redis). All pods share the same Redis key per merchantId. Atomic increment ensures accurate counting across instances.
common_gaps.detection_pattern: 10 pods, rate limit 100 req/min per merchant, but merchant can send 1000 req/min (100 × 10 pods)

key_takeaways: Token bucket: burst tolerance + smooth long-term rate control
Per-merchant (not global) buckets: isolate attack blast radius
Bucket4j: Java rate limiting library (token bucket, leaky bucket)
Redis-backed: required for correct distributed rate limiting across pods
429 Too Many Requests: standard HTTP response for rate limiting
Retry-After header: tell clients when to retry (RFC 7231)
X-RateLimit-* headers: inform clients of current limit and remaining
Idempotency keys: prevent duplicate payments on client retry after 429

self_check_questions.question: Why is per-merchant rate limiting better than global rate limiting for Razorpay?
self_check_questions.answer: Global rate limit (e.g., 100,000 req/min for all Razorpay API traffic): one attacker with a stolen API key can send 100,000 req/min → the global limit is hit → ALL merchants' legitimate requests are rejected. During a Diwali sale: Flipkart's payment requests are rate-limited because one attacker consumed the global budget. Per-merchant rate limit (100 req/min per API key): attacker's key hits 100 req/min limit → attacker gets 429 → all other merchants unaffected (each has their own bucket). Flipkart's Diwali traffic continues normally. Per-merchant isolation is the key: one bad actor cannot affect other legitimate users.

reflection.next_topic_preview: Module 23 Security is complete! You've mastered JWT (T23.1), OAuth2 (T23.2), Vault (T23.3), OWASP validation (T23.4), and Rate Limiting (T23.5). Module 24 covers System Design Patterns — CQRS, Event Sourcing, Saga Pattern, and Strangler Fig.
